lesson.name: Data Science Foundations - Lesson 2 — Statistical Backbone (refresher)

meta.lessonObjective: Refresh inferential statistics for data work.

messages.content:
Based on the provided text, here is a concise summary of the foundations of statistics:

### **Overview of Statistics**
Statistics is the science concerned with the collection, analysis, interpretation, and presentation of data. It is an essential toolkit for decision-making across diverse fields, including business, science, education, and law (p. 17).

### **Core Components**
*   **Descriptive Statistics:** Focuses on organizing and summarizing data through visual methods (such as graphs) or numerical calculations (such as averages) (p. 18).
*   **Inferential Statistics:** Uses formal methods and probability to draw conclusions about a population based on sample data. Effective inference requires sound procedures for generating "good" data and thoughtful examination of the results (p. 18).
*   **Probability:** A mathematical tool used to study randomness and the likelihood of an event occurring. Probability serves as the foundation for statistical inference, helping to determine the confidence level of our conclusions (p. 18).

### **Key Takeaways for Data Work**
*   **Conceptual Understanding over Calculation:** The primary goal of statistics is not the manual application of formulas, but understanding the data. Computational tools (calculators/software) should be used for the math, while the analyst provides the interpretation (p. 18).
*   **Data Quality:** Reliable insights depend on the ability to distinguish between "good" and "bad" data collection methods (p. 17).
*   **Context Matters:** Statistical interpretation often differs based on the population sampled. Differences in results (such as those observed in a classroom sleep-study exercise) are often driven by the nature and origin of the data collected (p. 17–18).

Lesson 2 — Statistical Backbone (refresher) - Presentation6 slides
Statistical Backbone: A Refresher
  • Foundations for data analysis and inference.
  • Reviewing essential concepts from sampling to testing.

This section provides an overview of OpenStax and its flagship textbook, *Introductory Statistics 2e*. Below is a summary of the key organizational and pedagogical aspects:

**Mission and Support**
OpenStax is a nonprofit initiative based at Rice University dedicated to improving educational access by providing free, peer-reviewed, and openly licensed textbooks. The project is sustained through partnerships with various philanthropic organizations, including the Bill & Melinda Gates Foundation, the National Science Foundation, and others.

**Textbook Structure and Flexibility**
*Introductory Statistics 2e* is designed for a one-semester introductory course for non-math and non-engineering majors. It emphasizes practical application over abstract theory. The content is released under a Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 license, allowing instructors to customize, remix, or assign specific sections based on their course needs. The text follows a standard sequence but offers the flexibility to adopt an alternate order, such as introducing linear regression earlier in the curriculum.

**Pedagogical Features**
The textbook integrates several tools to support student success:
- Examples: Strategic, step-by-step guidance for solving problems across diverse real-world topics.
- Try It: Practice problems designed to reinforce learning immediately after new concepts are introduced.
- Technical Integration: Extensive support for using TI-83, 83+, 84, and 84+ calculators, marked by a designated technology icon.
- Collaborative Exercises and Labs: Hands-on, in-class activities that encourage active learning, data gathering, and statistical analysis.
- Practice and Homework: A wide variety of problems ranging in difficulty to ensure mastery of the material.

**Accessibility and Updates**
The resource is available in free web and PDF formats, as well as low-cost print editions. The second edition has been revised for currency, accuracy, and inclusivity, with detailed errata management to ensure scientific precision. Instructors have access to additional resources, such as solution manuals and community hubs on OER Commons, to further supplement their teaching.

This section of the textbook, covering Chapters 2 through 3, focuses on descriptive statistics and foundational probability concepts. Below is a concise summary of the key points:

Descriptive Statistics (Chapter 2)

This chapter explains how to organize and interpret data sets using visual and numerical methods:

Visual Displays: Data can be represented through various graphical formats. Stemplots, line graphs, and bar graphs are useful for smaller data sets. Histograms and frequency polygons provide a way to visualize the distribution of continuous data. Time series graphs are essential for showing trends over specific periods. Box plots provide a visual summary of the data's distribution, highlighting the median, quartiles, and potential outliers.

Measures of Center: These metrics describe the central tendency of a data set. The mean is the arithmetic average, the median is the middle value when data is ordered, and the mode is the most frequently occurring value. The relationship between these three values determines the skewness of the data distribution.

Measures of Location and Spread: Quartiles and percentiles identify the location of specific data points. Measures of spread quantify the variability within the data. Key metrics include the range, variance, and standard deviation, which indicate how far data points deviate from the mean.

Probability Topics (Chapter 3)

This chapter introduces the fundamental rules and terminology of probability:

Terminology and Basic Rules: Probability is defined based on outcomes within a sample space. Key concepts include independent events, where the occurrence of one does not affect another, and mutually exclusive events, which cannot occur at the same time. The two basic rules of probability are that the sum of all probabilities in a sample space equals $1$ and the probability of any event $P(A)$ must satisfy $0 \leq P(A) \leq 1$.

Visual Aids for Probability: Contingency tables are used to organize data and calculate conditional probabilities. Tree diagrams and Venn diagrams serve as visual tools to map out complex relationships between events, helping to simplify calculations involving "and" (intersection) or "or" (union) scenarios.

This section, titled "Probability Topics," establishes the foundational work for quantifying uncertainty and understanding event relationships. The key points are summarized below:

**1. Probability Terminology**
Probability is a measure of the likelihood that an event will occur. Key terms include the sample space ($S$), which is the set of all possible outcomes, and an event ($A$), which is a subset of the sample space. The probability of an event $P(A)$ must satisfy $0 \leq P(A) \leq 1$.

**2. Event Relationships**
Two events are independent if the occurrence of one does not affect the probability of the other. Events are mutually exclusive if they cannot occur at the same time, meaning $P(A \cap B) = 0$.

**3. Basic Rules of Probability**
Two fundamental rules guide calculations:
- The Complement Rule: The probability of an event A not occurring is $P(A') = 1 - P(A)$.
- The Addition Rule: The probability of either of two events occurring is $P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)$.

**4. Conditional Probability and Contingency Tables**
Conditional probability calculates the likelihood of an event given that another has already occurred, denoted as $P(A|B) = P(A \cap B) / P(B)$. Contingency tables are used to organize data into rows and columns to analyze the relationship between two categorical variables, making the calculation of joint, marginal, and conditional probabilities easier.

**5. Visualizing Probabilities**
Tree diagrams provide a systematic way to map out sequences of events, especially helpful for multi-stage experiments. Venn diagrams use overlapping circles to visually represent the intersection, union, and complement of sets within a sample space, clearly showing the relationship between different events.

### Summary of Chapter 4: Discrete Random Variables (pp. 227–290)

This chapter focuses on discrete random variables, which represent outcomes that can be counted, and the probability distributions associated with them.

**1. Probability Distribution Functions (PDF)**
A discrete probability distribution function defines the probability for every possible value of a discrete random variable, $X$. It must satisfy two primary conditions:
* Each probability must be between 0 and 1, inclusive: $0 \le P(x) \le 1$.
* The sum of all probabilities must equal 1: $\sum P(x) = 1$.

**2. Expected Value and Standard Deviation**
The mean of a discrete random variable is known as the expected value, denoted by $\mu$ or $E(X)$. It represents the long-term average outcome of the variable:
$\mu = \sum x \cdot P(x)$

The standard deviation, $\sigma$, measures the spread of the data relative to the mean:
$\sigma = \sqrt{\sum (x - \mu)^2 \cdot P(x)}$

**3. Common Discrete Probability Distributions**
* **Binomial Distribution**: Used for experiments with a fixed number of independent trials, each with two possible outcomes (success or failure) and a constant probability of success $p$. The mean is $\mu = np$ and the standard deviation is $\sigma = \sqrt{np(1-p)}$.
* **Geometric Distribution**: Models the number of trials required to achieve the first success. If $p$ is the probability of success, the probability of the first success occurring on the $x^{th}$ trial is $P(X=x) = (1-p)^{x-1}p$.
* **Hypergeometric Distribution**: Applicable when sampling is done without replacement from a finite population. The probability reflects the number of successes in a sample drawn from a group containing both successes and failures.
* **Poisson Distribution**: Models the number of times an event occurs in a fixed interval of time or space, given a known constant average rate, $\mu$. The probability of $x$ occurrences is given by $P(X=x) = \frac{\mu^x e^{-\mu}}{x!}$.

**4. Experimental Applications**
The text concludes by reinforcing these concepts through practical experiments using playing cards and dice, demonstrating how theoretical probability distributions compare to empirical data collection.

### Summary of Chapter 5: Continuous Random Variables (Pages 291–334)

This chapter introduces the concept of continuous random variables, where outcomes are measured rather than counted, allowing for an infinite number of possible values within an interval.

**1. Continuous Probability Functions**
Unlike discrete variables, the probability of a continuous variable taking on an exact value is always zero. Instead, probabilities are calculated over specific intervals. The probability corresponds to the area under the probability density function (PDF) curve. For any PDF $f(x)$, the total area under the curve must equal 1, and the probability of an outcome between $a$ and $b$ is given by:
$P(a < X < b) = \int_{a}^{b} f(x) dx$

**2. The Uniform Distribution**
A continuous random variable has a uniform distribution if its values are spread evenly over the interval $[a, b]$. The PDF is constant:
$f(x) = \frac{1}{b - a}$ for $a \le x \le b$
Key characteristics include:
* Mean: $\mu = \frac{a + b}{2}$
* Standard Deviation: $\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{(b - a)^2}{12}}$

**3. The Exponential Distribution**
The exponential distribution is often used to model the time or space between random events (e.g., the time until the next customer arrives). It is characterized by a decay parameter $m$ (or $\lambda$).
* PDF: $f(x) = me^{-mx}$ for $x \ge 0$
* Mean: $\mu = \frac{1}{m}$
* Standard Deviation: $\sigma = \frac{1}{m}$
A defining property of the exponential distribution is its "memoryless" nature, meaning the probability of an event occurring in the next interval is independent of how much time has already elapsed.

**4. Applications and Computation**
The chapter concludes with exercises and resources for applying these distributions to solve problems involving time, distance, and measurement. The use of cumulative distribution functions (CDF) is emphasized as the primary method for finding probabilities $P(X \le x)$ for both uniform and exponential models.

### Summary of Chapter 6: The Normal Distribution

This chapter introduces one of the most important probability distributions in statistics: the normal distribution. It provides the framework for understanding bell-shaped data and prepares the foundation for inferential statistics.

**1. The Standard Normal Distribution**
The standard normal distribution is a specific type of normal distribution with a mean of 0 and a standard deviation of 1. It is denoted as $Z \sim N(0, 1)$. Z-scores are used to transform any normal distribution into the standard normal distribution using the formula:
$z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$
where $x$ is the value, $\mu$ is the population mean, and $\sigma$ is the population standard deviation.

**2. Using the Normal Distribution**
The normal distribution is continuous and symmetric. Its total area under the curve is always 1. To find probabilities for a generic normal distribution $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma)$, one must convert the value $x$ into a $z$-score and then use standard normal probability tables or technology to find the corresponding area under the density curve. Key properties include:
* The mean, median, and mode are all equal.
* The curve is symmetric around the mean.
* The total area under the curve is equal to 1.

**3. Practical Application**
The chapter provides practical context through experiments, such as analyzing lap times and the measurements of specific physical features (e.g., pinkie length). These examples illustrate how to apply the normal distribution to real-world data and emphasize the use of the empirical rule (the 68-95-99.7 rule) to estimate the percentage of data falling within specific intervals of the mean.

**4. Key Objectives**
* Understand the properties of the normal probability density function.
* Calculate $z$-scores to standardize data.
* Use normalization to solve for probabilities of specific ranges.
* Interpret the relationship between raw data observations and the normal curve.

### Summary of Chapter 7: The Central Limit Theorem (Pages 365–404)

This chapter introduces the Central Limit Theorem (CLT), one of the most fundamental concepts in statistics. It explains how the distribution of sample means behaves as the sample size increases, regardless of the shape of the original population distribution.

**1. The Central Limit Theorem for Sample Means**
The CLT states that if you draw sufficiently large samples from a population with mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$, the distribution of the sample means ($\bar{X}$) will be approximately normal. This holds true even if the original population is not normal. 
*   The mean of the sample means is equal to the population mean: $\mu_{\bar{x}} = \mu$.
*   The standard deviation of the sample means, known as the standard error, is: $\sigma_{\bar{x}} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$.
*   As the sample size $n$ increases, the normal approximation becomes more accurate.

**2. The Central Limit Theorem for Sums**
The CLT also applies to the sum of independent, identically distributed variables. If $n$ is large, the distribution of the sum ($\sum X$) will be approximately normal with:
*   Mean of the sum: $\mu_{\sum X} = n\mu$.
*   Standard deviation of the sum: $\sigma_{\sum X} = \sigma\sqrt{n}$.

**3. Practical Application**
The CLT allows statisticians to use the normal distribution to perform calculations involving sample means or sums, even when the underlying population distribution is unknown or non-normal. 
*   When calculating probabilities for sample means, the $z$-score formula is adjusted to account for the standard error: $z = \frac{\bar{x} - \mu}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}}$.
*   These calculations are essential for inferential statistics, as they form the basis for confidence intervals and hypothesis testing in subsequent chapters.

**4. Key Takeaways**
*   The sample mean ($\bar{x}$) is a random variable that follows its own distribution.
*   Greater sample sizes lead to a smaller standard error, making the distribution of the sample mean more concentrated around the population mean.
*   The "Rule of Thumb" is generally that a sample size of $n \geq 30$ is sufficient for the normal approximation to be effective, though this can vary based on the skewness of the original population.

Chapter 8, titled "Confidence Intervals" (pages 405–460), focuses on using sample data to estimate unknown population parameters. Since parameters such as the population mean ($\mu$) are often impossible to calculate directly, statisticians use sample statistics to create an interval estimate that is likely to contain the true parameter.

Key concepts in this section include:

Confidence Level
The confidence level represents the long-term success rate of the method used to construct the interval. A 95% confidence level implies that if we were to take many samples and build intervals in the same way, approximately 95% of those intervals would actually capture the true population parameter.

Confidence Interval for a Population Mean (Normal Distribution)
When the population standard deviation ($\sigma$) is known, the confidence interval is centered at the sample mean ($\bar{x}$). The formula includes a critical value ($z$) corresponding to the confidence level:
$CI = \bar{x} \pm z \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$

Confidence Interval for a Population Mean (Student's t-Distribution)
In most practical scenarios, $\sigma$ is unknown. We use the sample standard deviation ($s$) as an estimate and apply the Student's t-distribution, which accounts for the extra uncertainty. The degrees of freedom ($df$) are calculated as $n - 1$:
$CI = \bar{x} \pm t \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}}$

Confidence Interval for a Population Proportion
When dealing with categorical data where we measure the proportion of successes ($p'$), the confidence interval is constructed using the normal approximation:
$CI = p' \pm z \sqrt{\frac{p'(1-p')}{n}}$

Determining Sample Size
A critical application of this chapter is calculating the required sample size ($n$) to achieve a specific margin of error ($E$). By rearranging the confidence interval formula, one can determine exactly how many observations are needed to meet a desired precision:
$n = \frac{z^2 \sigma^2}{E^2}$

Key Takeaways
The margin of error ($E$) is inversely related to the sample size; as the sample size increases, the margin of error decreases. Additionally, a higher confidence level requires a wider interval, resulting in less precision in the estimate. Choosing between the z-distribution and the t-distribution depends primarily on whether the population standard deviation is known and whether the sample size is sufficiently large.

### Summary: Chapter 9 - Hypothesis Testing with One Sample (pp. 461-510)

This chapter introduces the fundamental process of inferential statistics known as hypothesis testing. It focuses on using sample data to make inferences about a single population parameter, such as the mean ($\mu$) or the proportion ($p$).

**1. Null and Alternative Hypotheses**
The process begins by defining two competing claims:
* The Null Hypothesis ($H_0$) represents the status quo or the assumption that there is no effect/difference.
* The Alternative Hypothesis ($H_a$) represents the claim the researcher seeks evidence for. It is usually expressed with inequalities such as $\neq, <,$ or $>$.

**2. Outcomes and Errors**
When testing hypotheses, two types of errors may occur:
* Type I Error: Rejecting a true null hypothesis. The probability is denoted by $\alpha$ (the significance level).
* Type II Error: Failing to reject a false null hypothesis. The probability is denoted by $\beta$.

**3. Decision Criteria and Methodology**
Hypothesis testing relies on identifying the appropriate probability distribution based on sample characteristics:
* The Normal Distribution ($Z$) is used when the population standard deviation ($\sigma$) is known or for large samples of proportions.
* The Student's t-distribution is used when $\sigma$ is unknown and the population is approximately normal.

To make a decision, researchers compare the calculated p-value to the chosen significance level ($\alpha$):
* If $p \text{-value} \leq \alpha$, reject $H_0$. 
* If $p \text{-value} > \alpha$, fail to reject $H_0$.

**4. The Testing Process**
The core workflow involves:
* Establishing $H_0$ and $H_a$.
* Determining the level of significance ($\alpha$).
* Collecting sample data to calculate the test statistic.
* Computing the p-value, which measures the strength of the evidence against $H_0$.
* Formulating a conclusion based on the comparison of the p-value and $\alpha$.

This statistical framework allows researchers to determine if observed variations in data are statistically significant or merely the result of random chance.

### Summary: Hypothesis Testing with Two Samples (Chapter 10)

This section focuses on statistical inference used to compare parameters from two distinct populations. The methods allow researchers to determine if observed differences between sample statistics are statistically significant or due to chance.

**1. Comparing Two Means**
*   **Unknown Standard Deviations:** When population standard deviations are unknown, the Student’s t-distribution is employed. If variances are assumed equal, a pooled variance approach may be used; otherwise, the Welch-Satterthwaite equation estimates the degrees of freedom.
*   **Known Standard Deviations:** If population standard deviations are known, the standard normal distribution ($Z$) is used to conduct the test.
*   **Matched or Paired Samples:** This design involves comparing two measurements taken on the same subject or matched pairs. The analysis reduces to a single sample test by examining the differences ($d_i$) between pairs, testing whether the mean difference ($\mu_d$) is equal to a hypothesized value (usually zero).

**2. Comparing Two Proportions**
*   This approach is used to test whether the proportions of successes in two independent populations are equal ($p_1 = p_2$). The test statistic typically involves the pooled proportion to estimate the common standard error under the null hypothesis.

**3. Decision Framework**
*   **Null Hypothesis ($H_0$):** Typically states there is no difference between the two parameters (e.g., $\mu_1 - \mu_2 = 0$ or $p_1 - p_2 = 0$).
*   **Alternative Hypothesis ($H_a$):** States a specific difference exists (greater than, less than, or simply not equal to the null value).
*   **Test Statistic:** Calculated based on the distribution (Z or t) to determine if the result falls into the rejection region defined by the chosen significance level ($\alpha$).
*   **Conclusion:** If the p-value is less than or equal to $\alpha$, the null hypothesis is rejected in favor of the alternative.

**Key Mathematical Considerations**
*   Calculations for the standard error of the difference between means rely on the specific standard deviations and sample sizes of both groups.
*   The use of paired data ($t = \frac{\bar{d} - \mu_d}{s_d / \sqrt{n}}$) is distinct from independent samples because it controls for variability between subjects, increasing the power of the test.
Here is a quiz for this lesson.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the core purpose of inferential statistics as presented in the textbook's scope?
options: To draw conclusions about a population based on sample data.
Solely to organize data using charts and graphs.
To prove a mathematical theorem with absolute certainty.
To calculate the exact mean and median of an entire population.
questions.explanation: Inferential statistics moves beyond descriptive summaries to make inferences or generalizations about a broader population from a smaller, representative sample.

questions.question: According to the Central Limit Theorem (Chapter 7), what happens to the distribution of sample means as the sample size increases?
options: It becomes identical to the population distribution.
It stops being a random variable and becomes a fixed constant.
It approaches a normal distribution, even if the underlying population is not normally distributed.
It becomes increasingly skewed, regardless of the population distribution.
questions.explanation: The Central Limit Theorem states that as the sample size gets larger, the distribution of the sample means will approach a normal distribution, regardless of the shape of the population distribution.

questions.question: What is the primary difference between a Type I and a Type II error in hypothesis testing?
options: A Type I error occurs when you fail to reject a false null hypothesis, whereas a Type II error occurs when you reject a true null hypothesis.
A Type I error involves a calculation mistake, while a Type II error is a conceptual misunderstanding.
A Type I error is rejecting a true null hypothesis, while a Type II error is failing to reject a false null hypothesis.
Type I errors only occur in small samples, while Type II errors only occur in large samples.
questions.explanation: In hypothesis testing, a Type I error is the 'false positive' (rejecting a true null), while a Type II error is the 'false negative' (failing to reject a false null).

questions.question: When assessing the strength and direction of a relationship between two quantitative variables, which statistical tool is most appropriate?
options: Chi-Square Test of Independence
One-Way ANOVA
Linear Regression and Correlation
Confidence Interval for a Population Proportion
questions.explanation: Chapter 12 covers Linear Regression and Correlation, which specifically analyzes how one variable changes in relation to another to determine the strength and direction of their association.

questions.question: Why is the distinction between descriptive and inferential statistics important for data work?
options: There is no practical difference; they both use the same formulas.
Descriptive statistics are used for past data, while inferential statistics are used for predicting future stock prices only.
Descriptive statistics are for math majors, while inferential statistics are for non-math majors.
Descriptive statistics provide a summary of provided data, while inferential statistics quantify the uncertainty involved in generalizing beyond that data.
questions.explanation: Descriptive statistics describe or summarize the characteristics of a dataset (the 'what is'), while inferential statistics help us make predictions or inferences about a population from a sample, accounting for the uncertainty inherent in sampling.

cards.front: What is the primary objective of the Central Limit Theorem in statistics?
cards.back: The Central Limit Theorem describes the distribution of sample means (or sums), stating that as sample size increases, the sampling distribution of the mean approaches a normal distribution, regardless of the shape of the population distribution.

cards.front: What is the difference between a Type I and Type II error in hypothesis testing?
cards.back: A Type I error occurs when the null hypothesis is rejected when it is actually true. A Type II error occurs when the null hypothesis is not rejected when it is actually false.

cards.front: When should you use the Student's t-distribution instead of the normal distribution for a confidence interval?
cards.back: The Student's t-distribution is used when estimating a population mean if the population standard deviation is unknown and the sample size is small or drawn from a normally distributed population.

cards.front: What does a Chi-Square goodness-of-fit test measure?
cards.back: It is used to determine if a sample data set matches a specific theoretical distribution, or if the observed frequencies in categories differ significantly from expected frequencies.

cards.front: What is the primary purpose of ANOVA (Analysis of Variance)?
cards.back: One-Way ANOVA is used to determine if there are statistically significant differences between the means of three or more independent groups.

cards.front: In linear regression, what does the correlation coefficient indicate?
cards.back: It indicates the strength and direction of a linear relationship between two quantitative variables.
A random variable that can take on only a countable number of distinct values (e.g., the number of heads in coin flips).

cards.front: What is the difference between independent and mutually exclusive events in probability?
cards.back: Independent events mean the occurrence of one does not affect the probability of the other. Mutually exclusive events mean both cannot occur at the same time.

cards.front: What is the purpose of a confidence interval?
cards.back: It provides a range of values, derived from sample data, that is likely to contain the true population parameter with a certain level of confidence.

cards.front: What does 'skewness' describe in a data set?
cards.back: Skewness describes the symmetry (or lack thereof) of the data distribution, specifically indicating whether the tail of the distribution extends more to the left (negative) or right (positive).

slides.title: Statistical Backbone: A Refresher
bullets: Foundations for data analysis and inference.
Reviewing essential concepts from sampling to testing.
slides.notes: Welcome to our refresher on the core components of statistical analysis. We will walk through the conceptual pillars that support your data work.

slides.title: Sampling and Data Fundamentals
bullets: Define statistics, probability, and key terms (p. 5).
Understand data collection and sampling variation (p. 10).
Organize data with frequency tables (p. 26).
Apply ethics in experimental design (p. 34).
slides.notes: Everything starts with how we collect and understand our data samples to ensure accuracy.

slides.title: Descriptive Statistics
bullets: Visualize data using stemplots and histograms (p. 66).
Measure central tendency: mean, median, and mode (p. 98).
Evaluate data spread and variance (p. 107).
Identify skewness and distribution location (p. 104).
slides.notes: Descriptive statistics allow us to summarize raw data into meaningful insights.

slides.title: Probability Frameworks
bullets: Grasp basic probability rules and terminology (p. 168).
Distinguish independent vs. mutually exclusive events (p. 172).
Use contingency tables for data relationships (p. 183).
Visualize outcomes with tree and Venn diagrams (p. 189).
slides.notes: Probability is the language of uncertainty which underpins all inferential methods.

slides.title: Random Variables and Distributions
bullets: Manage discrete variables and expected values (p. 228).
Apply common distributions: Binomial and Poisson (p. 237).
Analyze continuous probability functions (p. 292).
Utilize the Normal Distribution for standard modeling (p. 336).
slides.notes: Distributions categorize our data based on how outcomes vary across a set range.

slides.title: Inference: CLT to Hypothesis Testing
bullets: Apply the Central Limit Theorem to sample means (p. 366).
Estimate intervals for population parameters (p. 405).
Conduct hypothesis testing for one sample (p. 461).
Compare data sets using two-sample hypothesis testing (p. 511).
slides.notes: Finally, we use these tools to make rigorous conclusions about populations from our samples.
